buttress: A prop; a wall or abutment built archwise, serving to support another wall on the outside, when very high or loaded with a heavy superstructure.
To support by a buttress; to prop.
butts: A place where archers meet to shoot at mark. Also, short pieces of land in arable ridges and furrows.
butyraceous: Having the qualities of butter; resembling butter.
butyrous: Having the qualities of butter; resembling butter.
buy: pret. and pp. bought. pron. bawt.

To acquire the property, right or title to any thing, by paying a consideration or an equivalent in money. It differs from barter only in this, that in barter the consideration or equivalent is some species of commodity; in purchase, the consideration is money paid or promised. To purchase; to acquire by paying a price to the satisfaction of the seller; opposed to sell.
To procure by a consideration given, or by something that is deemed worth the thing bought; to procure at a price; as, to buy pleasure with praise; to buy favor with flattery.

To bribe; to corrupt or pervert the judgment, by paying a consideration. To buy off, to influence to compliance; to cause to bend or yield by some consideration, as to buy off conscience; to detach by a consideration given, as to buy off one from a party. To buy out, to buy off, or detach from.

To purchase the share or shares of a person in a stock, fund, or partnership, by which the seller is separated from the company, and the purchaser takes his place, as, A buys out B. To purchase stock in any fund or partnership, is to buy in. To buy on credit, is to purchase a thing, on a promise in fact or in law, to make payment at a future day. To buy the refusal, is to give money for the right of purchasing at a fixed price at a future time. To buy the small pox, in South Wales, is to receive it by inoculation. In popular language, to buy is to pay dear for, as in Chaucer.
buzz: To make a low hissing sound, as bees; to make the sound of z, with an expiration of breath between the tongue and the roof of the mouth or upper teeth.
To whisper; to speak with a low hissing voice; to make a low hissing sound.
To whisper; to spread, as report, by whispers, or to spread secretly.
The noise of bees; also, a whisper.
buzzard: A species of falco, or hawk, the buteo; a rapacious, but sluggish bird; the breast usually of a yellowish white; the upper parts of a deep brown. In some parts of America, it is called the great Hen- hawk, from its feeding on poultry.
buzzardet: A species of Falco or hawk, resembling the buzzard in most respects; but its legs are in proportion rather longer.
buzzer: A whisperer; one who is busy in telling tales secretly.
buzzing: Making a low hissing sound; whispering; tattling in secret.
by: Near; close; as, sit by me; that house stands by a river.

Near, in motion; as, to move, go or pass by a church. But it seems, in other phrases, or with a verb in the past time, to signify past, gone beyond. “The procession is gone by;” “the hour is gone by;” “John went by.” We now use past as an equivalent word. The procession is gone past. Gone by is in strictness tautology, as now used; but I apprehend by signifies primarily near.

Through, or with, denoting the agent, means, instrument or cause; as, “a city is destroyed by fire;” “profit is made by 6, commerce;” “to take by force.” This use answers to that of the Latin per, through, denoting a passing, acting, agency, or instrumentality. 0 66 ”
“Day by day;” “year by year;” “article by article.” In these phrases, by denotes passing from one to another, or each particular separately taken.
“By the space of seven years.” In this phrase, by denotes through, passing or continuing.
by-coffee-house: A coffee house in an obscure place.
by-concernment: An affair distinct from the main business.
by-dependence: An appendage; that which depends on something else, or is distinct from the main dependence.
by-end: Private end; secret purpose or advantage.
by-lane: A private lane, or one out of the usual road.
by-law: A town law; the law of a city, town or private corporation.
by-path: A private path; an obscure way.
by-speech: An incidental or casual speech, not directly relating to the point.
by-stander: One who stands near; a spectator; one who has no concern with the business transacting.
by-street: A separate, private or obscure street.
by-way: A secluded, private or obscure way. C, the third letter in the English alphabet, and the second articulation or consonant, is a palatal, nearly corresponding in sound with the Greek x, kappa, and with the HebrewIt bears a middle place in pronunciation, between the aspirate, and the palatal. It is a Roman character, borrowed from the Gr. x, or from the oriental, which was used in languages written from right to left, and when inverted and the corners rounded, becomes C. In the old Etruscan, it was written with the corners rounded, but not inverted; in Arcadian, C, as now written. That its sound in Latin was the same, or nearly the same, as that of kappa, may be known from the fact, that the Greeks, while the Latin was a living language, wrote kappa for the Roman C. Perhaps the same character may be the basis of the Arabic.
balsanm: An oily, aromatic, resinous substance, flowing spontaneously or by incision, from certain plants. A great variety of substances pass under this denomination. But in modern chimistry, the term is confined to such vegetable juices, as are liquid or spontaneously become concrete, and consist of a resinous substance, combined with benzoic acid, or capable of affording it by decoction or sublimation. The balsams are either liquid or solid; of the former, are the balm of Gilead and the balsams of copaiba, Peru and tolu; of the latter, benzoin, dragon’s blood, and storax. Balsam apple, an annual Indian plant; included under the genus Momordica. A water and a subtil oil are obtained from it, which are commended as deobstruents. BALSAM. Balsam tree. This name is given to a genus of plants called Clusia; to another, called Copaifera, which produces the balsam of Copaiba; and to a third, called Pis
battering-raim: In antiquity, a military engine used to beat down the walls of besieged places. It was a large beam, with a head of iron somewhat resembling the head of a ram, whence its name. It was suspended by ropes in the middle to a beam which was supported by posts, and balanced so as to swing backwards and forwards, and was impelled by men against the wall. It was sometimes mounted on wheels.
bean-fly: A beautiful fly, of a pale purple color, found on bean flowers, produced from a maggot called mida.
becalnm: To still; to make quiet; to appease; to stop, or repress motion in a body; used of the elements and of the passions; as, to becalm the ocean, or the mind. But calm is generally used. BECALM.
To intercept the current of wind, so as to prevent motion; to keep from motion for want of wind; as, highlands becalm a ship.
bedlanm: A mad house; a place appropriated for lunatics.
bedwarf: To make little; to stunt or hinder growth.
beliefs: In the heat of persecution, to which christian belief was subject, upon its first promulgation. BELIEF.

In some cases, the word is used for persuasion or opinion, when the evidence is not so clear as to leave no doubt; but the shades of strength in opinion can hardly be defined, or exemplified. Hence the use of qualifying words; as a firm, full or strong belief.
bells: To bear the bell, is to be the first or leader, in allusion to the bell- wether of a flock, or the leading horse of a team or drove, that wears bells on his collar. BELL. To shake the bells, a phrase of Shakespeare, signifies to move, give notice or alarm. BELL.
bellatrikx: A ruddy, glittering star of the second magnitude, in the left shoulder of Orion; so named from its imagined influence in exciting war.
bemoll: To bedraggle; to bemire; to soil or incumber with mire and dirt.
bewaill: To bemoan; to lament; to express sorrow for. It expresses deep sorrow; as, to bewail the loss of a child. The true penitent bewails his ingratitude to God. BEWAIL.
by-word: A common saying; a proverb; a saying that has a general currency.
byssus: The asbestus, composed of parallel fibers, is by some called by this name.
byssolite: A rare mineral, occurring in very delicate filaments, short, flexible and elastic. Their color is olive green, or brownish yellow, and their luster a little silky. Jameson places byssolite under actinolite; Hauy arranges it under amianthoid.
byzant: A gold coin of the value of fifteen pounds sterling, so called from being coined at Byzantium. Also, a piece of gold offered by the king on certain festivals.
byzantine: A gold coin of the value of fifteen pounds sterling, so called from being coined at Byzantium. Also, a piece of gold offered by the king on certain festivals.

Pertaining to Byzantium, an ancient city of Thrace situated on the Bosporus. In the year 330, Constantine the Great took possession of Byzantium, enlarged and embellished it, and changed its name to Constantinople.
